coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers as a fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive detection of genistein in soya products
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu-Zhen
Zhang, Bo
Chen, Qing-Qing
Qin, Lan
Hu, Ya-Qi
Bai, Chun-Hua
Li, Yi-Le
Qin, Bei
coredata.dc:description: Rapid detection of genistein in soya products has remained difficult. Current methods necessitate sample handling and use of costly instruments. Here, using a simple one-pot reverse microemulsion method, a sensor based on N-doped carbon dots conjugated molecularly imprinted polymers (N-CDs@MIPs) was synthesized to analyze genistein. N-doped carbon dots were used as fluorescent component, genistein as the template molecule, and molecularly imprinted polymers as the selective sorbent in this fluorescence sensor. The sensor was then examined and optical studies demonstrated that N-CDs@MIPs not only had strong fluorescence emission and outstanding optical stability, but also had good sensitivity (detection limit 35.7 nM) and selectivity to genistein. Furthermore, the N-CDs@MIPs materials were used to analyze genistein in soya products, and the findings (which ranged from 99.77% to 106.11%) show that the N-CDs@MIPs has high potential for quickly detecting the amount of genistein in complicated food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Genistein
Fluorescent sensor
Soya products

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Gen in real sample
Fluorescence measurement of Gen by N-CDs@MIPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education industry-University Cooperative Education
Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Yunnan Provincial Department of Education Science Research Fund Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Project fund of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JK0901), Young People fund of Xi’an Medical University (No. 2021QN22), Scientific and technological Innovation Team project of Xi’an Medical University (No. 2021TD03, No. 2021TD07), Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province (No. 2021ZDLSF03-05), Ministry of Education industry-University Cooperative Education Project (No. 202101021043).

This work was supported by the Scientific Research Project fund of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JK0901), Young People fund of Xi'an Medical University (No. 2021QN22), Scientific and technological Innovation Team project of Xi'an Medical University (No. 2021TD03, No. 2021TD07), Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province (No. 2021ZDLSF03-05), Ministry of Education industry-University Cooperative Education Project (No. 202101021043).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-CDs@MIPs.

A. TEM image of N-CDs. B. TEM image of N-CDs@MIPs. C. FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs. D. XPS spectrum of N-CDs and N-CDs@MIPs. E. XRD pattern of N-CDs and N-CDs@MIPs. F. TGA curves of (1) N-CDs and (2) N-CDs@MIPs.

A. The emission spectra of N-CDs@MIPs at various excitation wavelengths (from 300 nm to 400 nm). B. Excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs@MIPs. C. Emission spectra of N-CDs@NIPs (curve a), N-CDs@MIPs (without Gen, curve b) and N-CDs@MIPs (with Gen, curve c).

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs@MIPs at various concentrations of Gen (from 0 to 200 μM). B. Emission spectra of N-CDs@NIPs at various concentrations of Gen (from 0 to 200 μM). C. The relationship between F0/F of N-CDs@MIPs and the concentrations of Gen. D. The relationship between F0/F of N-CDs@NIPs and the concentrations of Gen. F0 and F were the synchronous fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@MIPs with and without Gen.

A. Selectivity of N-CDs@MIPs, N-CDs@NIPs for 40 μM Gen, 40 μM Kaempferol, 40 μM Ferulic Acid, 40 μM Caffeic acid, 40 μM Emodin. B. Anti-interference ability of N-CDs@MIPs, N-CDs@NIPs for 40 μM Gen, 20 μM Gen + 20 μM Kaempferol, 20 μM Gen + 20 μM Ferulic Acid, 20 μM Gen + 20 μM Caffeic acid, 20 μM Gen + 20 μM Emodin. C. Fluorescence responses of N-CDs@MIPs in the presence of Gen or metal ions (20 μM). D. Fluorescence responses of N-CDs@MIPs in the presence of Gen or metal ions (100 μM).
Gen analytical results in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers as a fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive detection of genistein in soya products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Institute of Medicine, Xi'an Medical University, Xi'an 710021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Institute of Medicine, Xi'an Medical University, Xi'an 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Chengdu Medical College, Chengdu 610500, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Chengdu Medical College, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid detection of genistein in soya products has remained difficult. Current methods necessitate sample handling and use of costly instruments. Here, using a simple one-pot reverse microemulsion method, a sensor based on N-doped carbon dots conjugated molecularly imprinted polymers (N-CDs@MIPs) was synthesized to analyze genistein. N-doped carbon dots were used as fluorescent component, genistein as the template molecule, and molecularly imprinted polymers as the selective sorbent in this fluorescence sensor. The sensor was then examined and optical studies demonstrated that N-CDs@MIPs not only had strong fluorescence emission and outstanding optical stability, but also had good sensitivity (detection limit 35.7 nM) and selectivity to genistein. Furthermore, the N-CDs@MIPs materials were used to analyze genistein in soya products, and the findings (which ranged from 99.77% to 106.11%) show that the N-CDs@MIPs has high potential for quickly detecting the amount of genistein in complicated food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Genistein (4′,5,7-trihydroxyisoflavone, Gen) is a typical phytoestrogen that can be found in a variety of dietary vegetables, including soya products, vegetables, meat substitutes and fruits (Lee, Kim, Kim, Yoo, & Kang, 2021; Yu, Rios, Castro, Liu, Yan, & Dixon, 2021). Various studies have revealed that Gen was most commonly consumed by humans in the form of soybeans or soya products and was used as an auxiliary medicine to treat women's diseases (Mamagkaki, Bouris, Parsonidis, Vlachou, Gougousi, & Papasotiriou, 2021). However, excessive intake of Gen can increase risk of hormone sensitive cancer, particularly postmenopausal breast cancer (Hu, Song, Gao, Nie, Eisenbrand, & Xie, 2014; This, Cremoux, Leclercq, & Jacquot, 2011; Zaineddin et al., 2012). And it would also result in infertility, breast tenderness, pedal edema, and nausea (Katarzyna et al., 2021; Seppen, 2012). Therefore, it's critical to detect and precisely quantify the amount of the limitation of ingestion Gen in food. Nevertheless, the content of Gen in food is trace, and the food matrix is complex, making it difficult to accurately identify the contents of Gen. To date, several techniques for determining Gen have been reported, including liquid chromatography (LC) (Aresta, Cotugno, Massari, & Zambonin, 2017), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Nalewajko-Sieliwoniuk, Hryniewicka, Jankowska, Kojło, Kamianowska, & Szczepański, 2020; Saha, & Kroon, 2020), gas chromatographic-tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS/MS) (Ferrer, Barber, & Thurman, 2009) and an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Sakamoto, Yusakul, Pongkitwitoon, Paudel, Tanaka, & Morimoto, 2015). Although these methods can accurately determine Gen, a large number of real samples were required for efficient pretreatment and preconcentration prior to instrumental analysis (Ji, Zhao, & Zhao, 2022). Furthermore, these methods have low selectivity and take a long time (Xie, Luo, Na, Zhang, & Zong, 2021). Thus, developing a simple, efficient, sensitive, and time-saving method to detect Gen in food samples is critical.

The unique optical and electronic properties of quantum dots have sparked the interest of many people (Ghani, Rezaei, Jamei, & Ensafi, 2020). Quantum dots are highly luminescent nanoparticles with a wide and continuous absorption spectrum, size-dependent optoelectronic properties, and high photoluminescence intensity. (Chen, Liu, Li, Xing, & Huang, 2021). However, they are toxic, expensive, and have low selectivity (Xu, Chen, & Ma, 2018). In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) has been become an entrancing carbon material due to low toxicity, low cost, good water-solubility, resistance to photobleaching and tunable surface functionalities (Hu et al, 2021; Vijeata, Chaudhary, & Chaudhary, 2022). Moreover, CDs' superior properties set them apart from traditional quantum dots and organic dyes, making them promising candidates for bioimaging (Sharma, Tiwari, & Mobin, 2017), drug delivery (Gui, Zhang, Chen, Yu, & Ma, 2017), and live cell imaging (Belza, Opletalová, & Poláková, 2021). Many factors contribute to the increased fluorescence intensity of CDs through heteroatom doping or surface functionalization (Yang et al., 2018). More importantly, doped heteroatoms can tune the optical properties and unique electronic properties of CDs, resulting in higher fluorescence quantum yields and abundant surface functional groups (Hu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2018). Among these doped CDs, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was most common and easiest to prepare (Zhou, Ashley, Feng, & Sun, 2018). Zhu et al., for example, proposed a simple and high-output method for creating of N-CDs, with a quantum efficiency of up to 58% (Zhu et al., 2013). However, N-CDs have no selectivity to limit their development (Xu et al., 2018).

Currently, coating N-CDs with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) is gaining popularity as a way to improve N-CDs selectivity (Andrade Teixeira, Fonseca Dinali, Leijoto de Oliveira, da Silva, & Bastos Borges, 2021; Sun et al., 2019). MIPs are synthetic polymers that can recognize target compounds using recognition sites that are predetermined by template molecules based on shape, size, and functional groups (Zhang, Wang, He, Li, & Zhang, 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). MIPs were a good platform for a variety of sensor applications because of their great selectivity, stability, and identification (Ali & Omer, 2022). Amiri and colleagues used CDs and MIPs as sensing materials to detect ceftazidime, demonstrating that MIPs can be used as selective materials for ceftazidime detection at the trace level (Amiri, Faridbod & Zoughi, 2021). Cui et al. described a florescence probe based on CDs coated with MIPs for detecting cetirizine in saliva and urine samples that was extremely selective and sensitive (Cui et al., 2022). It is reasonable to believe that the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs exhibited high sensitivity and specificity owing to the ‘‘key and lock” model. It's encouraging that there haven't been any reports of Gen detection using N-CDs and molecularly imprinted technology up until now.

The goal of this research was to create a fluorescence sensor (N-CDs@MIPs) that can detect Gen in dietary food in an ultrasensitive and highly selective manner. The fluorescence sensor in this paper was formed by using Gen as the template molecule, N-CDs as the fluorophore, and MIPs as selective sorbents. Various characterization methods, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and so on, were used to describe and discuss the characterization of N-CDs@MIPs. Furthermore, the sensor's sensitivity and selectivity to Gen were thoroughly investigated. Finally, the fluorescence approach based on N-CDs@MIPs was effectively employed to detect Gen in soya products with satisfactory recoveries.

Gen, kaempferol, ferulic acid, caffeic acid and emodin were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with purities greater than 98%. (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), citric acid, ethylenediamine and n-hexanol were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co (St. Louis, USA). Triton X-100 and poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride solution (PDDA, 35%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cyclohexane, acetone, ethanol, methanol, ammonia and acetic acid were obtained from Tianjin Hongyan Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The aforementioned chemical reagents were all analytically pure.

The fluorescent measurements were recorded using F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) was used for the TEM. XPS spectrum were detected by using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system (ThermoFisher, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-TR) were received by using Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by TGA/DSC 1 (TG 209F3, NETZSCH, Germany) with a rate of temperature increase from room temperature to 800◦C in a nitrogen environment of 10◦C⋅min−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were recorded using X’Pert PRO MPD (Panalytical, Holland). UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary60, Agilent, USA) was used to observe the UV–vis absorption spectra.

By using the described hydrothermal process, N-CDs were created (Zhu et al., 2013). After dissolving 8.4056 g citric acid in 80 mL of ultra-pure water, 2.68 mL of ethylenediamine was gradually added while being constantly stirred to create a homogeneous solution. After that, the solution was put into a stainless steel vessel and heated to 200◦C for 5 h. Finally, dialysis and lyophilization were used to purify the brown solution, yielding N-CDs powder for further usage.

N-CDs@MIPs was created utilizing modified reverse microemulsion method (Chen, Liu, Li, Xing, & Huang, 2021). In a 50 mL flask, 1.80 mL n-hexanol, 8.0 mL cyclohexane and 1.80 mL Triton X-100 were mixed for 20 min while stirring. The above solution was then treated with N-CDs (5 mg/mL). After stirring 10 min, 1 mmol TEOS, 100 uL ammonia and 100 uL 0.05% (mass fraction) PDDA aqueous solution were added one by one to the mixture, which was then stirred at room temperature. Subsequently, 0.1 mmol Gen and 0.5 mmol APTES were dispersed in 0.5 mL n-hexanol and pre-polymerized for 2 h before adding to the above solution. The reaction system was then sealed and stirred continuously for 12 h at room temperature. After polymerization, the 10 mL acetone was added to the mixture to cut off the microemulsion. The resulting particles was centrifuged and washed three times with ethanol. After that, the N-CDs@MIPs was washed with a 9:1 methanol/acetic acid solution until the Gen was no longer detectable by UV–vis spectra. Finally, the product was rinsed with ethanol and dried for 5 h.
N-doped carbon dots coupled with non-imprinted polymers (N-CDs@NIPs) was created as a control by using the same process without Gen.

With an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, a fluorescence intensity between 390 and 640 nm was attained. The following conditions applied to each measurements: the slit width was 10 nm and the scan speed was 1200 nm min−1. 5 mg of N-CDs@MIPs or N-CDs@NIPs was dispersed in 50 mL ultra-pure water (pH = 7.5). Then, the cuvette was filled with 1 mL working solution and certain volumes of Gen standard solution (4 mM). The fluorescence values of maximum emission wavelength when Gen was absent and present, denoted as F0 and F, respectively. The values of F0/F were plotted in relation to different concentrations of Gen (0.4–200 µM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Gen in real sample

ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the N-CDs@MIPs method was investigated using a variety of soya products from local supermarkets. For analysis, the products were divided into four categories for analysis: soya, dried bean curd sticks, soymilk powder, and soybean milk. To begin, the sample was comminuted and dried right away. The solid item samples were then sonicated for 30 min in a 25 mL 70% ethanol solution. After three repetitions, the supernatant was collected and incubated in a 2 mol·L-1 HCl solution at 85 °C. In addition, the solution's pH was brought down to 7.0 using a 40% NaOH solution. The hydrolysate was extracted three times with an equal volume of diethyl ether before drying in a rotary vacuum evaporator. After that, the residue was then redissolved in 2 mL ethanol solution and kept at 4 °C for future experiments.

Certain volumes of samples were mixed with 100 μg·mL−1N-CDs@MIPs solution (1 mL, pH = 7.5) to determine the concentration of Gen in real samples. Furthermore, the recoveries of Gen in soya products were calculated by adding various concentration of Gen standard solution. The fluorescence intensity of 440 nm as the maximum emission spectrum were used to calculate Gen. The concentrations of Gen in soya products will then be calculated using the linear equation. Additionally, to further confirm the reliability of this method, the soya products were also measured by HPLC.

The preparation of polymers was depicted in Fig. 1 . Ethylenediamine, being a popular doping heteroatom, can boost CDs' fluorescence effectiveness as well as their affinity to analytes by providing numerous functional groups (Luo et al., 2018). The emission wavelength of N-CDs was 440 nm, while Gen had no peak at this emission wavelength. Therefore, N-CDs was selected as fluorescent component. In this study, the N-CDs@MIPs was constructed through simple one-pot reverse microemulsion method. In mixed system, the cyclohexane was applied as the continuous phase, and Triton X-100 and n-hexanol served as surfactant and co-surfactant, respectively. As a functional monomer, APTES can provide N-CDs and template molecule (Gen) with enough –NH2 surface binding sites. To increase the stability of N-CDs, TEOS acted as a cross-linking agent by depositing a layer of SiO2 there. It also collaborated with the prepolymerization system to generate N-CDs@MIP (Peng et al., 2021). Furthermore, to achieve superior fluorescence response of N-CDs@MIPs, some essential factors, such as the molar ratio of Gen to APTES to TEOS and the concentration of N-CDs were explored in Supplementary Information Fig. S1. The best molar ratios for Gen to APTES and TEOS were 1:5:10, while the ideal concentration of N-CDs was chosen to be 5 mg·mL−1.

TEM was used to examine the morphologies of N-CDs and N-CDs@MIPs. The N-CDs was spherical with diameter of about 3.0 nm, as seen in Fig. 2 A. The rough spherical structure of the composite material exhibited in Fig. 2B indicates that the polymers were formed. The polymer particles were larger in size compared to N-CDs.

Fig. 2C depicts the FT-IR spectra of three different materials. The blue curve shows the various characteristics of N-CDs. It can be found that stretching vibrations of C-OH at 3427.37 cm−1 and CH at 2922.04 cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1641.36 cm−1. It can also be noted that the asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations of C–NH–C and NH were at 1568.06 cm−1 and 1095.52 cm−1, respectively (Zhu et al., 2013). These findings demonstrated the presence of various hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino group configurations on the N-CDs surface (Luo et al., 2018). As seen in red curve of Fig. 2C, the obvious absorption peaks at 1139.30, 1051.20 and 780.87 cm−1 were respectively referred to as the Si-O-Si, SiOC asymmetric stretching and Si-O symmetrical stretching (Ghani, Rezaei, Jamei, & Ensafi, 2020). Moreover, the spectra of N-CDs@NIPs and N-CDs@MIPs were comparable. These bands confirmed that the imprinting layer have been efficiently grafted on the surface of N-CDs (Xu et al., 2018).

XPS confirmed the element composition of N-CDs and N-CDs@MIPs, as seen in Fig. 2D. Peaks at 282.08, 397.56, and 532.49 eV were derived from C1s, N1s, and O1s, separately, for the N-CDs XPS curve. Compared to the XPS curve of N-CDs@MIPs, it was clearly observed that a new peak of Si2p at 100.92 eV (Xu et al., 2018). The outcomes demonstrated the successful synthesis of N-CDs@MIPs. In addition, the atomic% and PP At.% were described in Supplementary Information Table S1 and Supplementary Information Table S2. It can be obviously seen that the contents of O1s of polymers were higher than those in N-CDs, while, on the contrary, the levels of C1s in N-CDs@MIPs was lower. This conclusion can further confirm the successful preparation of polymers.

Further, XRD patterns of prepared materials were reported in the 2θ = 10–80˚ range. As shown in Fig. 2E, one relatively discernible diffraction peak at 2θ around 24˚ were observed in two curves, and the peak positions could be indexed to the C (002) plane (Ghani, Rezaei, Jamei, & Ensafi, 2020). Those results indicate that N-CDs have amorphous structures. In addition, this also suggested that modification of MIPs did not change crystalline structure of N-CDs (Zhang et al., 2020).

The thermostability of N-CDs and N-CDs@MIPs were investigated using TGA. TGA thermograms of two different materials, as shown in Fig. 2F, found two weight loss stages in the 25–800 °C range. The first-stage weight loss was around 10.95% and 9.59% at 180 °C for the curves of fluorescent material and N-CDs@MIPs, respectively. This decomposition was due to the elimination of the free water and residual solvent. When the temperature was raised to 800 °C, N-CDs experienced a weight loss of 53.77%, while N-CDs@MIPs experienced a weight loss of 20.67%. This could be because the surface imprinting layer increased the polymers' thermal stability. And it was also demonstrated that the polymers had a high degree of heat stability.

Fig. 3 depicts findings of optical performance study of material. According to Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@MIPs increased first and then decreased as excitation wavelength changes between 300 and 400 nm. It was decided that the ideal excitation wavelength was 340 nm. In particular, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength was 340 nm and 440 nm of N-CDs@MIPs, as illustrated in Fig. 3B. The above optical performance of polymers was similar to N-CDs characterized (Supplementary Information Fig. S2). This indicates that the imprinting layer does not restrict the photoluminescence properties of N-CDs (Xu et al., 2018). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@NIPs was comparable to that of N-CDs@MIPs (without Gen), both of which were stronger than N-CDs@MIPs (without Gen), as seen in Fig. 3C. Those results shown that polymers had good fluorescence properties and the templates were almost removed from the recognition cavities of composite material. Meanwhile, the fluorescence stability of imprinted polymers was also studied. As illustrated in Supplementary Information Fig. S3, fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@MIPs changed little over 240 min and 10 days, indicating that the polymers had superior optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement of Gen by N-CDs@MIPs

ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the best detection conditions, some assay conditions including dispersion solvents of N-CDs@MIPs, quantity of N-CDs@MIPs, pH level and ion intensity were investigated. The dispersion solvent was an important parameter affecting the fluorescent intensity. As shown in Supplementary Information Fig. S4A, under identical experimental circumstances, fluorescent intensity of different dispersion solvents (including methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone and water) exhibited great differences, and the highest quenching ratio was obtained when polymers in aqueous solution. Thus, water was chosen as the dispersion solvent. Following that, the influence of different amount of N-CDs@MIPs (5–300 μg/mL) was recorded in water (Supplementary Information Fig. S4B). It was discovered that quenching response was increased by increasing the quality of N-CDs@MIPs from 5 to 100 μg/mL. Thus, the concentration of 100 μg/mL was used for further study. Additionally, a range of 2.5–10.5 pH was studied as a significant impact. The results (Supplementary Information Fig. S4C) revealed that the quenching efficiency was greatest at pH 7.5. The quenching ratio declined when pH was lower than 7 because the amine groups on the surface of composites may be protonated leading to decreases hydrogen bonding ability between the Gen and APTES (Amiri, Faridbod & Zoughi, 2021). Moreover, the value of F0/F had a slightly decreased at alkaline pH, most probably due to the silica shell and the binding sites can cause damage and be ionized (Amiri, Faridbod & Zoughi, 2021). Thus, pH 7.5 was selected for more testing. In the meantime, the impart of solution ion strength on the fluorescence intensity was recorded by varying NaCl concentrations from 0 to 2 M. The quenching efficiency of N-CDs@MIPs was significantly stable with increase of ionic strength, as shown in Supplementary Information Fig. S4D. This phenomenon indicates that the composite materials detection Gen could be not affected by the ionic strength.

The fluorescence properties of N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs was assessed under ideal conditions at various concentrations of Gen (0–200 µM). As shown in Fig. 4 A and 4B, upon addition same concentration of Gen, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@MIPs was reduced obviously compared to N-CDs@NIPs. The different quenching effects might be due to more binding cavities on N-CDs@MIPs. Then, the linear relationships between F0/F and Gen concentration were shown in Fig. 4C. The equation of F0/F = 0.0252C1 + 1.048 (R2 = 0.9953) was fitted for the lower level of Gen ranging from 0.4 to 50 µM. However, as for higher level of Gen (50–200 µM), the graph of N-CDs@MIPs was measured as F0/F = 0.0733C2 -1.938 (R2 = 0.9896). The ununiformity of N-CDs@MIPs recognition sites could be related to the two linear relationships (Yang et al., 2018). As a control, the response of N-CDs@NIPs to Gen was linear with two different linear relationships (Fig. 4D). The linear equation F0/F = 0.0190C1 + 0.9970 (R2 = 0.9978) was fitted for lower level (2–50 µM), while F0/F = 0.0488C2 -0.5957 (R2 = 0.9925) was suitable for higher level (50–200 µM). Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) for N-CDs@MIPs was also expected by using 3σ/K equation, in which σ was the blank standard deviation (n = 10) and K was the slope of the calibration equation for the lower Gen level. Thus, the LOD for N-CDs@MIPs was 35.7 nM.

The selectivity of polymers were investigated to detect Gen and other components, including kaempferol, ferulic acid, caffeic acid and emodin. These chemicals were the analogues of Gen in chemical structure as shown in Supplementary Information Fig. S5. As seen in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence response of N-CDs@NIPs was almost identical for all analogues and Gen. This nonspecific adsorption capacity of N-CDs@NIPs might be due to the strong adsorption ability of the SiO2 coats. Nevertheless, it was obvious that the fluorescence response to Gen was prominent than other compounds. The different fluorescence response was mainly because of specific recognition cavities of N-CDs@MIPs. Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of N-CDs@MIPs was also tested and the result was shown in Fig. 5B. It can be seen that a slight interference for the quenching efficiencies of Gen was observed from kaempferol, ferulic acid, caffeic acid and emodin, respectively. However, the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of was far less than that of Gen at the same concentration. As a result, the N-CDs@MIPs implementation of Gen identification has a good specificity.

Furthermore, some potential metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+) were evaluated in order to further analyze the anti-interference of polymers. As seen in Fig. 5C and D, fluorescence response of polymers to metal ions at high and low concentrations varied very little, demonstrating that metal ions have essentially no effect on N-CDs@MIPs fluorescence. As a result, it was further evidence that N-CDs@MIPs had superior specificity.

Stability and reproducibility were also important characteristics in the application of polymers. The composite to Gen stability was examined by measuring changes in fluorescence response on a daily basis for 10 days. As shown in Supplementary Information Fig. S6A, there was no significant difference between the 10 days fluorescent data, suggesting that the N-CDs@MIPs possessed sufficiently stable. Moreover, in order to assess reproducibility of prepared polymers, eight batches of polymers were undertaken, and the values of F0/F were recorded. It can be obviously seen from Supplementary Information Fig. S6B that the fluorescence quenching efficiencies have no significant different for different batches of composite materials. Those results indicated that polymers have brilliant reproducibility.

Following the findings in Supplementary Information Fig. S7, the quenching mechanism of polymers to template was examined. It can be seen that there was no additional absorbance peak after mixing N-CDs@MIPs with Gen (Fig. S7A). This proved that physisorption was what caused Gen to bind to the surface of polymers (Yang et al., 2018). Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs@MIPs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Gen were shown in Fig. S7B. It can be found that the absorption spectrum of Gen dose not overlap with excitation or emission spectra of polymers, which proved the quenching mechanism was not fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) (Xu, Chen, & Ma, 2018). However, the template Gen can form a Meisenheimer complex with specific recognition sites of N-CDs@MIPs through hydrogen bonding. The above results indicate that the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be due to the synergistic effect of electron transfer. The fluorescence of N-CDs@MIPs was quenched as a result of electrons transferring from excited N-CDs@MIPs electrons to lowest empty molecular orbital of Gen during its adsorptive and binding to the imprinted layer interactions (Hu et al., 2021; Amiri, Faridbod & Zoughi, 2021).

In this research, real samples of soya, dried bean curd sticks, soymilk powder, and soybean milk were used, and the influences of Gen and selected real samples on fluorescence response were measured. Supplementary Information Fig. S8 shows that Gen and the soya products have no affect on fluorescence of N-CDs@MIPs. Thus, the real samples were used for further experiment. As listed in Table 1 , the concentration of Gen in the soya was 21.67 ± 1.05 µM, the dried bean curd sticks was 1.08 ± 0.51 µM, the soymilk powder was 80.76 ± 0.91 µM, and the soybean milk was 15.77 ± 1.46 µM. The real samples were spiked with the right amounts of Gen. The results indicated that the recoveries for soy products were attained 99.77%-106.11% with RSD of 0.13–5.03%. Furthermore, the HPLC method was used to validate the developed method. Supplementary Information Fig. S9 shown the HPLC results of real samples. And as shown in Supplementary Information Table S3, there was no significant difference between the fluorescence analysis method and the HPLC method. Thus, this findings demonstrated that N-CDs@MIPs has good accuracy for use in Gen detection in food samples.

Compared with some reported methods of detecting Gen in Supplementary Information Table S4, the LOD values acquired by current approach were greater than HPLC methods without long analysis time. Surprisingly, the LOD of this method was on par with the LOD of GC–MS/MS. Although LC-MS/MS and ELISA had lower detection limits, they require expensive equipments, highly skilled personnel and highly expensive antibody. Our proposed sensor exhibits an appropriate detection limit with sufficient sensitivity to detect Gen of soya products. As a result, the designed N-CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor had the potential to detect Gen quantitatively in foods.

This research created a novel and efficient N-CDs@MIPs fluorescence sensor for specific detection of template. Optical characteristics, detecting conditions optimization, selectivity experiments, stability and repeatability experiments, and the quenching mechanism were all explored. The above results demonstrated that the N-CDs@MIPs exhibited strong fluorescence emission, long-term stability, outstanding sensitivity (LOD of 35.7 nM) and remarkable selectivity. Furthermore, the polymers were used to detect Gen in soya products quickly and accurately. The findings showed that produced sensor was a useful fluorescent sensor for monitoring Gen in complicated food samples.

Yu-Zhen Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Bo Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Qing-Qing Chen: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Lan Qin: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ya-Qi Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Chun-Hua Bai: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yi-Le Li: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Bei Qin: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Project fund of Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JK0901), Young People fund of Xi’an Medical University (No. 2021QN22), Scientific and technological Innovation Team project of Xi’an Medical University (No. 2021TD03, No. 2021TD07), Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province (No. 2021ZDLSF03-05), Ministry of Education industry-University Cooperative Education Project (No. 202101021043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136133.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
